nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral reefs with mixed sand and rubble patches and Acropora coral stands; reported from roughly 4–30 m on outer reef slopes and lagoon margins

decorPreferences: deep fine sand bed (5–8 cm) for nocturnal burying
mature live rock with abundant microfauna
open swimming space across the tank's footprint
tightly sealed lid with no gaps around equipment

diet.notes: In the wild, the New Guinea Tamarin Wrasse has been documented feeding on benthic invertebrates — small crustaceans, copepods, amphipods, foraminiferans, and other hard-shelled micro-prey grazed from live rock and sand. In captivity, most specimens need a mature live-rock system with active copepod and amphipod populations to sustain the initial transition period; the majority eventually accept vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped krill, and other small meaty foods. Three or more small feedings per day are generally advised, especially in the weeks after introduction, and a connected refugium is commonly recommended to keep microfauna populations topped up. Their pharyngeal-jaw morphology produces a distinctive chew-spit-rechew feeding pattern that is characteristic of the genus.

goodWith: tangs and surgeonfish introduced after the wrasse
other peaceful wrasses of different genera
avoidWith: other male Anampses in standard-sized aquaria
ornamental shrimp, small crabs, fanworms, and clams

summary: A striking black-backed, white-spotted Western Pacific tamarin wrasse generally considered expert-only; needs a mature reef system, deep fine sand, and a tightly sealed lid.
careNotes: The New Guinea Tamarin Wrasse is one of the more frequently imported Anampses species, but the genus as a whole is regarded as expert-only in the aquarium hobby. Shipping stress and acclimation mortality have been widely reported, with new arrivals often refusing prepared foods for the first days or weeks; targeted feedings of live brine shrimp, live black worms, or live copepods are commonly used to coax initial feeding, after which most specimens transition to vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp. A mature tank with abundant live-rock microfauna (copepods, amphipods, small worms) is generally considered essential, and a connected refugium is commonly recommended to help sustain pod populations through the first months. Like other Anampses, this species sleeps buried in the substrate and will dive into the sand when startled — a fine sand bed of at least 5 cm (ideally 7.5 cm or deeper) is strongly advised. Tamarin wrasses are also expert jumpers; a tightly fitted lid with no equipment gaps is essential. Reef compatibility is generally rated 'with caution' — the species is harmless to corals but has been reported to consume ornamental shrimp, small crabs, fanworms, and may pick at clam mantles. Anampses are protogynous hermaphrodites, typically kept as a single specimen or as a harem of one male with several females; multiple males in a single tank tend to fight. The species is sexually dichromatic: females display a black-backed, white-spotted body with three blue-rimmed eyespots, while terminal males develop more elaborate facial markings and yellow or orange highlights.
breedingNotes: The New Guinea Tamarin Wrasse is oviparous, spawning in distinct pairs — a pattern shared across the family Labridae. Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby. As a protogynous hermaphrodite (a fish that begins life as female and may later change sex to male), wild populations typically form small loose groups of females attended by a dominant male.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Used as primary because LiveAquaria product pages (both the New Guinea Tamarin Wrasse and Black-backed Wrasse SKUs) returned HTTP 404. Confirmed scientific name, family Labridae / order Eupercaria, max size 20.0 cm SL, depth range 4–30 m, Western Pacific distribution (Philippines and Taiwan to Fiji, north to the Izu Islands, south to the GBR and New Caledonia; recently Tonga), preferred temperature 24.6–29.3 °C (mean 28.4), trophic level 3.5, oviparous with distinct pairing, and the harem social structure (small groups of females with a dominant male).

additional.name: FishKeeper UK — New Guinea Wrasse
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (20 cm), water parameters (23–27 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), sand-bed depth (≥7.5 cm / 3 in ideal), jumping behavior, reef compatibility ('with caution' — eats ornamental shrimp, snails, fanworms, picks at clams), incompatibility with dragonets, and the 'one per tank or mated pair / harem' rule. Listed minimum tank size in the 251–500+ L range with a strong note that 1000 L is preferable for harems.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia — Anampses neoguinaicus
additional.notes: Cross-checked minimum tank volume (500 L / 132 gal), max size (20 cm / 7.9 in), Western Pacific distribution, sand-bed requirement (≥5 cm / 2 in), 'difficult to keep alive' rating, multiple feedings per day requirement (especially when newly added), shipping sensitivity, and the strong jumping/escape risk. Diet documented as small crustaceans (krill/mysis/artemia) plus zooplankton.

additional.name: LiveAquaria Species Spotlight: Tamarin Wrasses of the Genus Anampses
additional.notes: Genus-wide reference: confirms LiveAquaria's 'Expert Only' rating for the genus, the deep soft substrate requirement (≥2 in / 5 cm), the chew-spit-rechew feeding behavior driven by pharyngeal-jaw morphology, and the pearl-white body / black back / three blue-rimmed eyespots color description for A. neoguinaicus. Note: both species-level LiveAquaria product URLs (the New Guinea Tamarin Wrasse SKU and the Black-backed Wrasse SKU 3002) returned HTTP 404 on 2026-06-14, so this genus article is used in place of a species product page.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby. The species is oviparous with distinct pairing during spawning and is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning individuals begin life as females and the dominant fish in a group can transition to male. Wild populations form harem social structures consisting of several females attended by a single dominant male.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: The New Guinea Tamarin Wrasse carries high shipping mortality and frequently refuses prepared foods during the initial acclimation period. It depends on a mature reef system with abundant live-rock microfauna and a deep fine sand bed for nocturnal burying, poses a serious escape risk, and requires multiple small feedings per day. It is not appropriate for new or undersized reef systems.
